summary: Mark Zuckerberg outlines the transition of Facebook to Meta, emphasizing the shift toward the 'metaverse'—an embodied internet focused on a feeling of presence. He reflects on past lessons, stressing that building loved products isn't enough and that privacy, safety, and open standards must be foundational. He also critiques closed platforms, advocating for low-cost, accessible technology that maximizes the creative economy.

principles.statement: We don't build services to make money; we make money to build better services.
principles.rationale: This approach allows the company to support very large and long-term investments necessary to build fundamentally better services for users.
principles.source_quote: we don’t build services to make money; we make money to build better services.

principles.statement: Building products people love isn't enough.
principles.rationale: The internet brings new voices and ideas, but also new challenges, risks, and disruptions. Privacy, safety, and open standards must be built in from the beginning to manage these risks.
principles.source_quote: One of the main lessons I’ve learned is that building products people love isn’t enough.

principles.statement: Services should be accessible to as many people as possible by making them cost less, not more.
principles.rationale: Keeping costs low or free allows billions of people to use the services and hundreds of millions of businesses to rely on them, which maximizes the overall creative economy.
principles.source_quote: We want our services to be accessible to as many people as possible, which means working to make them cost less, not more.

principles.statement: Technology should be built around people interacting with each other, not just people interacting with technology.
principles.rationale: The ultimate dream of social technology is feeling truly present with another person, which requires designing technology around human connection rather than just utility.
principles.source_quote: While most tech companies focus on how people interact with technology, we’ve always focused on building technology so people can interact with each other.

mental_models.name: The Embodied Internet
mental_models.description: Viewing the next generation of the internet not as a flat screen you look at, but as an immersive environment you are inside of, characterized by a deep feeling of presence.
mental_models.example: Teleporting instantly as a hologram to be at the office without a commute, or sitting in your parents' living room to catch up despite physical distance.

mental_models.name: The 'Meta' (Beyond) Mindset
mental_models.description: The belief that there is always more to build and a next chapter to the story, moving beyond current constraints of screens, distance, and physics.
mental_models.example: Rebranding the company to Meta to reflect moving beyond just social media apps into building the foundational technologies for the metaverse.

heuristics: Privacy and safety need to be built into the metaverse from day one.
Sell devices at cost or subsidized to make them available to more people.
Offer developer and creator services with low fees to maximize the creative economy.

signature_quotes.text: The next platform will be even more immersive — an embodied internet where you’re in the experience, not just looking at it.
signature_quotes.context: Describing the fundamental nature of the metaverse.

signature_quotes.text: Feeling truly present with another person is the ultimate dream of social technology.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining the core motivation behind building the metaverse.

signature_quotes.text: While most tech companies focus on how people interact with technology, we’ve always focused on building technology so people can interact with each other.
signature_quotes.context: Defining the company's unique DNA and approach to product development.

signature_quotes.text: From now on, we will be metaverse-first, not Facebook-first.
signature_quotes.context: Announcing the strategic pivot and rebranding of the company.

anti_patterns.description: Operating closed platforms with a lack of consumer choice and high fees for developers.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It stifles innovation and holds back the broader internet economy.

anti_patterns.description: Treating privacy, safety, and open standards as afterthoughts.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: The internet's evolution brings new risks and disruptions of established interests; failing to build these in from day one leads to negative societal impacts.
